chapter_context.summary: Chronicles Jonah's second call and his prompt obedience in entering Nineveh, delivering a brief five-word Hebrew sermon of doom ('Yet forty days, and Nineveh shall be overthrown'), which triggers a comprehensive national repentance from the king to the beasts, leading God to relent of the threatened disaster.

outline.text: The Second Call and Jonah's Three-Day Journey into Nineveh with the Message of Overthrow
outline.explanation: This section focuses on The Second Call and Jonah's Three-Day Journey into Nineveh with the Message of Overthrow (3:1-4), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Jonah.

outline.text: National Repentance: Nineveh's Sackcloth, Fasting, and the King's Proclamation of Urgent Prayer and Turning from Evil
outline.explanation: This section focuses on National Repentance: Nineveh's Sackcloth, Fasting, and the King's Proclamation of Urgent Prayer and Turning from Evil (3:5-9), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Jonah.

outline.text: Divine Compassion: God Seeing Their Works and Relenting of the Planned Calamity
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Divine Compassion: God Seeing Their Works and Relenting of the Planned Calamity (3:10), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Jonah.

key_themes.title: The Universality of Divine Mercy
key_themes.description: Jonah shows that God's grace and compassion are not limited to one covenant nation (Israel) but extend to all humanity, including foreign enemies like Assyria.

key_themes.title: The Futility of Fleeing God
key_themes.description: The narrative makes clear that God's sovereignty is absolute over creation, storm, fish, plant, and worm, rendering human attempts to escape His call completely futile.

devotional.question: Reflecting on the events and outcomes in Chapter 3, what does Jonah's reaction to God's mercy tell us about our own tendency to hoard grace and judge others?
How can you cultivate a heart that is aligned with God's compassion for the 'great cities' and lost people in our modern world?

cultural_historical.title: Nineveh and the Assyrian Empire
cultural_historical.text: Nineveh was the magnificent and brutal capital of the Assyrian Empire. To Israel, Assyria was a cruel, terrifying, and hated oppressor, which explains Jonah's extreme reluctance to preach a message that might lead to their salvation.
